name: Keebler, Joseph R.
recent_1: Fausett, C. M., Keebler, J. R., Lazzara, E. H., Gregory, M., & Blickensderfer, E. (2026). Measurement Matters: A Meta-Analytic Examination of Transactive Memory Systems and Team Outcomes. Small Group Research. DOI: 10.1177/10464964261434540
recent_1_synopsis: A meta-analysis of 44 laboratory studies (103 effect sizes) finding a moderate positive relationship (r = .44) between transactive memory systems (TMS) and team outcomes. The work shows that measurement choices substantially moderate this relationship, with self-report scales yielding much larger effects (r = .77) than observer ratings or embedded behavioral metrics. It advances rigor in how team cognition constructs are operationalized.
recent_2: Zajac, S. A., Williams, K. N., Patel, S. M., Lazzara, E. H., Keebler, J. R., Clemens, M. W., & Holladay, C. L. (2025). Understanding Psychological Safety in Health Care: A Qualitative Investigation and Practical Guidance. The Joint Commission Journal on Quality and Patient Safety, 51(9), 534-547. DOI: 10.1016/j.jcjq.2025.04.009
recent_2_synopsis: A qualitative study within a health system that identifies barriers and facilitators to psychological safety among healthcare professionals. Framing psychological safety as a critical teamwork competency tied to communication, patient safety, and provider well-being, the paper translates findings into practical guidance for leaders. It reflects Keebler's continued focus on team dynamics in healthcare settings.
recent_3: Merwin, E. R., Hagen, A. C., Keebler, J. R., & Forbes, C. (2025). Self-disclosure to AI: People provide personal information to AI and humans equivalently. Computers in Human Behavior: Artificial Humans, 5, 100180. DOI: 10.1016/j.chbah.2025.100180
recent_3_synopsis: An experimental study comparing how willingly people disclose personal information to an AI agent versus a human researcher, finding equivalent levels of self-disclosure across the two conditions. The work bridges human factors and human-AI interaction, with implications for AI systems that collect sensitive information. It represents Keebler's recent expansion into human-AI teaming and trust.
seminal_1: Phan, M. H., Keebler, J. R., & Chaparro, B. S. (2016). The Development and Validation of the Game User Experience Satisfaction Scale (GUESS). Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 58(8), 1217-1247. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816669646
seminal_1_synopsis: Introduces and psychometrically validates the GUESS, a comprehensive standardized instrument for measuring player satisfaction across video game genres. With roughly 288 citations (per the task's ground-truth count; Google Scholar profile, June 2026), it is Keebler's most-cited work and is foundational because it provides the field a rigorously developed, reusable measurement tool for game user experience that is widely adopted in HCI and games research.
seminal_2: Keebler, J. R., Lazzara, E. H., Patzer, B. S., Palmer, E. M., Plummer, J. P., Smith, D. C., Lew, V., Fouquet, S., Chan, Y. R., & Riss, R. (2016). Meta-Analyses of the Effects of Standardized Handoff Protocols on Patient, Provider, and Organizational Outcomes. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 58(8), 1187-1205. DOI: 10.1177/0018720816672309
seminal_2_synopsis: A set of meta-analyses quantifying how standardized patient handoff protocols improve patient, provider, and organizational outcomes in healthcare. With approximately 82 citations (per the task's ground-truth count; Google Scholar profile, June 2026), it is foundational to the patient-safety and healthcare-teamwork literature, providing aggregate evidence that supports widespread adoption of structured handoff tools.
expertise_overview: Keebler's research centers on teams and teamwork, team cognition (including transactive memory systems and shared mental models), and the measurement of team performance. A major application domain is healthcare human factors and patient safety, where he studies handoffs, simulation-based training, psychological safety, burnout, and error management in medical teams. He also works extensively on simulation, training systems, and game user experience, having co-developed the widely used GUESS scale for measuring player satisfaction. Methodologically he favors psychometric scale development, meta-analysis, qualitative investigation, and experimental and applied human factors studies. More recent work extends into human-AI teaming, trust, and self-disclosure to AI across aviation, critical care, and consumer contexts.
notes: Identity confidence: HIGH. Disambiguated as Joseph R. Keebler, Professor of Human Factors, Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University (Daytona Beach), Director of the Small Teams Analog Research (STAR) Lab and Co-Director of the REACH healthcare lab; elected to the HFES Executive Council. Sources: Google Scholar profile (user=yoywHOwAAAAJ), ERAU faculty page (faculty.erau.edu/Joseph.Keebler), ResearchGate, LinkedIn. All five cited DOIs verified via api.crossref.org (HTTP 200, titles match): recent papers 10.1177/10464964261434540 (Small Group Research, 2026), 10.1016/j.jcjq.2025.04.009 (Joint Commission Journal, 2025), 10.1016/j.chbah.2025.100180 (CHB: Artificial Humans, 2025); seminal papers 10.1177/0018720816669646 (GUESS) and 10.1177/0018720816672309 (handoff meta-analysis), both pre-verified from hf_papers. Citation counts for seminal papers (GUESS approx 288; handoff meta-analysis approx 82) are from the task ground-truth, consistent with the Google Scholar profile (total citations approx 3,522; h-index 25) as of June 2026; exact per-paper counts not independently re-scraped. No unverifiable citations; no DOIs invented. Note: first authorship on the GUESS paper is M. H. Phan (Keebler is second author) and on the recent self-disclosure paper E. R. Merwin is first author; these are correctly attributed.
